_meta.source: BYKI Mobile V2 — lib/engine/rules/ (all 7 system analyzers)
_meta.description: Complete rule set from V2's 7-system health scoring engine. Each rule contributes a weighted penalty to its system's health score. Health score = 100 − Σ(matched rule weight × 100). Rules are additive within systems.
_meta.language: Dart 3.12+ (transcribed to JSON for V5 use)

rule_structure.name: Descriptive name
rule_structure.description: Technical explanation of what this rule detects
rule_structure.thresholds: Numeric values used in the rule

special_notes.tropicalClimateOffset: Cooling system rules have +5°C on all temperature thresholds for Malaysian tropical climate
special_notes.oilRules: Oil system has NO direct oil quality PID — all rules are behavioral inference from other PIDs
special_notes.transmissionRules: Transmission rules use TCM-specific DIDs (Mode 22/23) not standard OBD Mode 01 — requires Rust agent

charging.description: Monitors alternator, battery, and charging circuit health
charging.healthScoring: 100 minus sum of triggered rule weights × 100

rules.name: Low Running Voltage
rules.description: Battery voltage below normal range while engine is running — charging system not maintaining proper voltage
possibleCauses: Weak battery failing to hold charge
rules.physicsNote: A healthy charging system maintains 13.5–14.8V while running. Below 12.9V indicates the alternator is not providing sufficient current above battery open-circuit voltage.

rules.name: Low Resting Voltage
rules.description: Battery voltage below 12.3V at idle — indicates discharged or failing battery
rules.physicsNote: Resting open-circuit voltage: 12.6V = 100% charge, 12.4V = 75%, 12.2V = 50%, 12.0V = 25%. Below 12.3V at idle is concerning.

rules.name: Low Charging Voltage
rules.description: Voltage at cruise RPM below 13.5V — alternator not meeting demand
rules.physicsNote: At cruise RPM, alternator output should comfortably exceed battery terminal voltage, providing 13.5–14.8V. Consistently below 13.5V at 1500+ RPM indicates alternator capacity issue.

rules.name: Overcharging
rules.description: System voltage exceeding 14.8V — voltage regulator failure risk of battery damage
possibleCauses: Voltage regulator stuck at full field
rules.physicsNote: Above 14.8V risks electrolyte boil-off in flooded batteries, accelerated grid corrosion. AGM batteries tolerant to ~14.9V. Above 15V risks electrical component damage.

rules.name: Flat Voltage vs RPM
rules.description: Voltage not increasing from idle to cruise — indicates flat alternator curve (possibly diode failure)
rules.triggerConditions: Voltage at RPM 2000 approximately equals voltage at RPM 800 (variance < 0.3V)
rules.physicsNote: A healthy alternator produces incrementally higher voltage as rotor speed increases (field current amplification). Flat response indicates rotor, stator, or rectifier issue.

rules.name: Voltage Fluctuation
rules.description: Voltage oscillating by more than 0.5V repeatedly — indicates intermittent contact or failing regulator
rules.triggerConditions: Voltage oscillates > 0.5V within short time window at stable RPM
rules.physicsNote: Voltage ripple is normal at <0.2V (AC component from rectified 3-phase). Visible fluctuations >0.5V indicate intermittent connection or regulator hunting.

rules.name: Voltage Drop Under Load
rules.description: Voltage drops below 12.5V when electrical loads activated — alternator cannot sustain demand
rules.triggerConditions: Voltage < 12.5V during high electrical load events (measured indirectly via load PID correlation)
possibleCauses: Battery unable to assist during peaks
rules.physicsNote: Alternator current output is RPM-dependent. At low RPM with high electrical demand (AC, headlights, heated seats), battery must supplement alternator. Voltage drop indicates insufficient capacity.

combustion.description: Monitors ignition quality, fuel combustion completion, O2 sensor activity, and misfire events

rules.name: LTFT Deviation
rules.description: Long-term fuel trim persistently positive or negative beyond ±10% — indicates chronic mixture offset
possibleCauses: Lean: Vacuum leak, low fuel pressure, clogged injector, MAF under-reading
Rich: Leaking injector, EVAP purge fault, MAP sensor offset
rules.physicsNote: LTFT is a learned offset stored in ECU memory. It compensates for systematic fueling errors by shifting base fuel map. Persistent deviation indicates a base calibration error the ECU is compensating for.

rules.name: STFT Instability
rules.description: Short-term fuel trim exhibiting large variance — indicates unstable mixture supply
rules.triggerConditions: STFT variance > 8% span in recent history
possibleCauses: Dirty throttle body causing mixture oscillation
rules.physicsNote: STFT is the real-time closed-loop correction. High variance indicates the ECU is making large, rapid corrections suggesting an unstable input (O2 signal or fuel supply).

rules.name: STFT Spike
rules.description: Short-term fuel trim spiking beyond ±15% momentarily — indicates acute fueling event
rules.physicsNote: ECU trim authority is typically ±25%. Spikes to ±15% indicate the ECU is near its correction limit, briefly. Fuel delivery drastically deviated from commanded.

rules.name: Active Misfire Detected
rules.description: OBD misfire counter above zero — unburned fuel being pushed into exhaust
possibleCauses: Compression loss (valve, piston ring, head gasket)
Ignition timing extreme out of range
rules.physicsNote: OBD misfire monitoring uses crank acceleration pattern. Each correctly fired cylinder produces measurable crankshaft acceleration pulse. Missing pulses = misfire. Each misfire pushes raw hydrocarbons into catalyst, risking thermal damage.

rules.name: Idle RPM Instability
rules.description: RPM at idle exhibiting oscillation — hunting idle indicates unstable combustion or idle control issue
rules.triggerConditions: RPM variance > 100 RPM at closed-throttle idle
rules.physicsNote: Idle stability is maintained by IAC (bypass air) and fuel trim cooperation. Hunting indicates a perturbation input (air leak or variable vacuum) that the idle control cannot dampen quickly enough.

rules.name: O2 Sensor Stuck Lean
rules.description: Upstream O2 sensor voltage persistently below 0.2V for > 10 seconds — sensor contaminated or stuck
rules.triggerConditions: O2 voltage < 0.2V sustained AND closed loop
possibleCauses: O2 sensor contaminated with silicone or coolant
rules.physicsNote: A narrowband O2 sensor should switch between ~0.1V (lean) and ~0.9V (rich) in closed loop. Stuck low indicates either real severe lean condition or sensor failure preventing ECU from seeing rich excursion.

rules.name: O2 Sensor Stuck Rich
rules.description: Upstream O2 sensor voltage persistently above 0.8V — ECU commanded rich or sensor stuck
rules.triggerConditions: O2 voltage > 0.8V sustained AND closed loop
possibleCauses: Large MAP/MAF sensor error causing over-fueling
rules.physicsNote: Sustained rich reading causes ECU to pull fuel trim negative. If trim hits -25% limit and mixture is still rich, indicates a fuel delivery override or sensor malfunction.

rules.name: O2 Low Variance (Lazy Sensor)
rules.description: Upstream O2 not switching actively — voltage variance below threshold indicating lazy/degraded sensor
rules.triggerConditions: O2 voltage variance < 0.3V over 30-second window in closed loop
possibleCauses: Aged O2 sensor with slow element response
O2 heater circuit failure causing cold sensor
rules.physicsNote: Healthy O2 sensors switch 3-8 times per second in closed loop at idle. Low variance indicates sensor is not cycling, meaning ECU cannot properly read lean/rich transitions needed for fuel control.

fuel.description: Monitors fuel delivery system: injectors, fuel pump, pressure regulation, and mixture formation

rules.name: Persistent Lean LTFT
rules.description: LTFT persistently positive beyond +10% — ECU learned that base fueling is lean
possibleCauses: Low fuel pressure reducing injection quantity
rules.physicsNote: LTFT positive = ECU adding fuel on top of base map because mixture runs lean. Consistent lean LTFT indicates a systematic un-metered air or under-fuel condition.

rules.name: Persistent Rich LTFT
rules.description: LTFT persistently negative beyond -10% — ECU learned base fueling is rich
possibleCauses: Leaking fuel injectors adding excess fuel
Saturated EVAP canister purging excess HC
MAF sensor over-reading causing excess fueling
rules.physicsNote: LTFT negative = ECU reducing fuel to compensate for systematic over-rich condition. Common after EVAP canister saturation or injector o-ring leaks.

rules.name: STFT Spikes
rules.description: STFT exhibiting sudden spikes beyond ±20% — acute fuel delivery event
rules.physicsNote: ECU trim limits are typically ±25-30%. Spikes near limit indicate the ECU is at maximum correction. If air/fuel ratio still wrong at trim limits, misfire or DTC is imminent.

rules.name: Lean Under Load
rules.description: Positive fuel trim increasing specifically under high load — fuel system cannot meet demand
possibleCauses: Weak fuel pump failing under demand
Pressure regulator failing under high flow
rules.physicsNote: Fuel pump must maintain pressure under peak flow demands. Restriction causes pressure drop under high injection pulse widths needed for WOT.

rules.name: Lean Persistence
rules.description: LTFT trending lean over multiple drive cycles — progressive lean condition developing
rules.triggerConditions: LTFT trending positive across sessions above 8%
rules.physicsNote: LTFT is session-persistent (stored in KAM). Progressive increase across sessions indicates a worsening fuel delivery or air metering problem.

rules.name: Rich Persistence
rules.description: LTFT trending rich over multiple drive cycles — progressive over-fueling condition
rules.triggerConditions: LTFT trending negative across sessions below -8%
rules.physicsNote: Negative LTFT trending progressively indicates worsening over-fueling. Often caused by injector o-ring degradation or cracked fuel pressure regulator diaphragm.

rules.name: O2 and Trim Correlation Lean
rules.description: High positive LTFT combined with O2 readings confirming lean mixture — multi-signal lean confirmation
possibleCauses: Low fuel pressure with MAF reading correctly
rules.physicsNote: When both LTFT and O2 signal confirm lean, this eliminates sensor disagreement and confirms actual lean mixture in cylinder.

rules.name: Bank 2 Lean Deviation
rules.description: Bank 2 LTFT significantly leaner than Bank 1 — bank-specific fuel delivery issue
possibleCauses: Intake manifold gasket leak on B2 side
Exhaust manifold crack leaking air before O2 sensor
VVT phaser issue on B2 bank
rules.physicsNote: Bank differential above 5-7% is a strong indicator of a bank-specific air or fuel issue, rather than a global fuel delivery problem.

cooling.description: Monitors thermostat, coolant circulation, radiator, and thermal management efficiency. All temp thresholds include +5°C offset for Malaysian tropical climate.

rules.name: Slow Warm-Up
rules.description: Engine not reaching at least 75°C within 10 minutes of start — thermostat stuck open
rules.physicsNote: A thermostat should start opening at ~88°C and be fully open by ~102°C. Engine that doesn't reach 75°C after 10 minutes has severely impaired thermal management — will run over-rich and reduce fuel economy.

rules.name: Low Warm-Up Rate
rules.description: Engine temp rising too slowly even before thermostat opens — indicates low coolant or water pump issue
rules.triggerConditions: ECT rise rate < 0.1°C/sec during initial warm-up
rules.physicsNote: Normal pre-thermostat warm-up rate is 0.2-0.5°C per second depending on ambient temp and idle load. Below 0.1°C/s indicates the coolant mass is absorbing heat very slowly — possibly large air pocket or low level.

rules.name: Overheating
rules.description: Coolant temperature above 110°C — engine in danger zone
rules.physicsNote: Above 105°C risks coolant boiling (if cap failed), aluminum alloy annealing, head gasket distortion. At 115°C, ECU should trigger cooling measures or shutdown protection.

rules.name: Elevated Temperature
rules.description: Temperature in warning zone 100-110°C — not yet critical but heating beyond design point
possibleCauses: Towing / heavy load on hot day
rules.physicsNote: The elevated range indicates cooling system is marginal. Under normal conditions it recovers, but sustained operation risks progressive degradation.

rules.name: Temperature Fluctuation
rules.description: Coolant temperature oscillating >10°C repeatedly at steady state — thermostat or circulation issue
possibleCauses: Coolant level low causing intermittent circulation
Water pump impeller slipping on shaft
rules.physicsNote: Normal thermostat operation causes ±3-5°C oscillation. Fluctuations above 10°C indicate the thermostat is opening/closing too aggressively or coolant flow is intermittent.

rules.name: Suboptimal Operating Temperature
rules.description: Engine running below optimal 82-95°C window even when warm — efficiency impact
possibleCauses: Cold ambient temperature with heavy air flow
rules.physicsNote: Optimal combustion efficiency occurs at 90-95°C where fuel atomization, oil viscosity, and catalytic converter efficiency are all optimal. Below 82°C engine runs enriched, increasing fuel consumption and wear.

rules.name: Cruise Temperature Drop
rules.description: Temperature dropping significantly when speed increases — thermostat stuck open allowing excessive coolant flow
rules.triggerConditions: ECT drops > 15°C when speed increases from idle to cruise
possibleCauses: Cooling fan running continuously at high speed
rules.physicsNote: With thermostat stuck open, increased coolant flow at higher pump speed (RPM/speed) allows radiator to over-cool the coolant. Temperature should be stable across speed ranges with a functioning thermostat.

emission.description: Monitors catalytic converter efficiency, O2 sensor pair activity, and EGR function

rules.name: Post-Cat O2 Mirroring Pre-Cat
rules.description: Downstream O2 switching at similar frequency to upstream — catalyst not storing oxygen
rules.triggerConditions: Downstream O2 switching rate > 70% of upstream rate
rules.physicsNote: A healthy catalyst uses its cerium oxide oxygen storage to buffer lean/rich spikes. Post-cat O2 should see this buffered, stable exhaust. If it mirrors upstream, storage capacity is exhausted — catalyst is depleted.

rules.name: Reduced O2 Storage Capacity
rules.description: Post-cat O2 responding too quickly to upstream changes — catalyst storage depleted
rules.triggerConditions: Time lag between upstream and downstream O2 response < 2 seconds
rules.physicsNote: Oxygen storage capacity (OSC) is the key catalyst performance metric. Healthy catalyst has 0.5-2g oxygen storage. Depleted catalyst shows < 0.1g — measured indirectly by response speed.

rules.name: Low Upstream-Downstream Voltage Difference
rules.description: Average voltage difference between upstream and downstream O2 too small
possibleCauses: Both O2 sensors reading similar values — catalyst transparent
Downstream sensor biased to upstream value
rules.physicsNote: Upstream sensor swings ±0.5V around 0.5V. Downstream held stable by catalyst should read ~0.55-0.65V. Small or reversed difference indicates catalyst not performing its storage function.

rules.name: Post-Cat O2 Unstable
rules.description: Downstream O2 exhibiting large unexplained oscillation — catalyst degraded or exhaust leak
rules.triggerConditions: Downstream O2 variance > 0.2V at steady state
rules.physicsNote: Downstream O2 should be stable (±0.05V) except during deliberate rich/lean transitions for OSC testing. Instability indicates variable exhaust composition reaching the sensor.

rules.name: Downstream O2 Stuck High
rules.description: Post-cat O2 permanently high (>0.7V) — running rich through catalyst or sensor fault
possibleCauses: Engine running very rich through catalyst
rules.physicsNote: Post-cat persistent high voltage indicates sustained rich exhaust gas. Healthy catalyst should consume excess hydrocarbons, normalizing exhaust gas lambda.

rules.name: Downstream O2 Stuck Low
rules.description: Post-cat O2 permanently low (<0.3V) — sensor failure or exhaust leak before downstream sensor
possibleCauses: O2 sensor heater failure keeping element cold
Exhaust leak ahead of downstream sensor adding O2
rules.physicsNote: Post-cat sensor stuck lean is most often a failed heater circuit, preventing sensor element from reaching operating temperature (350°C minimum for zirconia cells).

rules.name: High LTFT with Degraded Catalyst
rules.description: Both lean LTFT and catalyst degradation signals present — combined emissions efficiency concern
rules.triggerConditions: LTFT > 10% AND downstream O2 mirroring upstream
possibleCauses: System running lean (high HC, low CO) AND catalyst unable to oxidize remaining HC
rules.physicsNote: Lean condition increases NOx emission even as HC decreases. Degraded converter no longer reduces NOx effectively. Combined — all three major pollutants (HC, CO, NOx) at risk.

rules.name: Lambda Upstream Deviation
rules.description: O2 upstream average not centering around stoichiometry (0.5V on NBO2 = Lambda 1.0)
rules.triggerConditions: O2 upstream 30s average outside 0.4-0.6V in closed loop
rules.physicsNote: In closed loop, ECU drives mixture toward stoichiometric Lambda=1.0 (AFR 14.7:1 gasoline). O2 should oscillate around 0.5V on a conventional NBO2. Bias indicates trim limits reached or sensor error.

oil.description: Behavioral oil degradation inference — NO direct oil quality PID. Rules infer oil stress from thermal, speed, RPM, and usage patterns. Used to predict oil change urgency.
oil.note: All rules are behavioral inference. Oil health is estimated, not directly measured.

rules.name: Thermal Stress Accumulation
rules.description: Coolant temperature persistently high — oil experiencing thermal degradation above 110°C
possibleCauses: Cooling system dysfunction allowing oil to overheat
rules.physicsNote: Oil degradation rate doubles for every 10°C above normal operating range (Arrhenius rule). Above 120°C, base oil oxidation accelerates exponentially, reducing interfacial film strength and increasing viscosity breakdown.

rules.name: High RPM Operation Stress
rules.description: Sustained high RPM operation stressing oil film at bearing surfaces
possibleCauses: Spirited driving above oil design load
rules.physicsNote: At high RPM, hydrodynamic oil film at bearings must withstand increasing centrifugal and shear forces. High-shear viscosity stability (HTHS) is the key metric — oils meeting SAE J300 HTHS > 3.5 cP are preferred.

rules.name: Excessive Idle Accumulation
rules.description: High proportion of running time spent at idle — reduces oil film pressure and allows fuel/water dilution
rules.triggerConditions: Idle fraction > 40% of run time (RPM < 900 AND throttle < 5%)
rules.physicsNote: At idle, oil pump barely maintains minimum pressure. Coolant and oil temperatures may not fully burn off condensation and fuel blow-by, causing dilution. Short-trip/idle-heavy cycles are particularly hard on oil.

rules.name: Mileage-Based Stress Indicator
rules.description: Accumulated distance since inferred oil change — approaching service interval
rules.triggerConditions: Inferred distance since last service > 7000 km
rules.note: V5 should track session-cumulative distance if no external service record is available
rules.physicsNote: Conventional oil is typically rated for 5000-7500 km. Full synthetic 10000-15000 km. Base rule uses 7000 km conservative threshold for Malaysian tropical conditions.

rules.name: Short Trip Pattern
rules.description: Regular short trips preventing oil from reaching full operating temperature — causes accelerated contamination
rules.triggerConditions: ECT never exceeds 80°C within a session AND run time > 10 min
possibleCauses: Urban driving with stops before full warm-up
rules.physicsNote: Below ~85°C engine operating temperature, water and fuel vapors condense into oil rather than evaporating out. Short trip duty cycle is identified by industry as the most damaging to oil condition.

rules.name: Combined High Stress
rules.description: Multiple stress factors active simultaneously — accelerated oil degradation probability
rules.triggerConditions: Three or more of: heat stress, high RPM, long idle, long distance all triggers active concurrently
rules.physicsNote: Oil degradation is multiplicative, not additive under multiple stressors. High temp + high load + extended interval = oil life severely shortened beyond linear interpolation.

rules.name: Malaysian Tropical Idle Penalty
rules.description: Idle penalty adjusted for Malaysian climate — AC compressor load increases idle thermal stress beyond standard thresholds
rules.triggerConditions: Idle fraction > 30% in high ambient temperature environment
rules.note: Tropical climate (Malaysia-specific): AC compressor adds ~3-5 kW parasitic load at all times, raising idle ECT by 5-8°C vs temperate baseline
rules.physicsNote: In Malaysia, air conditioning is mandatory in virtually all vehicles. AC creates additional idle load that temperate-climate oil change intervals don't account for. This rule applies an offset reducing the idle threshold trigger.

rules.name: Direct Oil Temp High
rules.description: Direct oil temperature reading exceeding safe operating limit (when PID 0x015C available)
rules.physicsNote: Direct oil temperature measurement is available on modern vehicles via OBD-II PID 0x015C. Above 130°C is critical — API service limit for most engine oils. Immediate enrichment and thermal protection strategies recommended.

transmission.description: Monitors automatic/CVT transmission health using TCM-specific parameters (Mode 22 DIDs via UDS/ISO-TP, requires Rust agent). Cannot be read via standard Chrome GATT OBD Mode 01.

rules.name: Transmission Fluid Overheating
rules.description: Transmission fluid temperature at critical overheating level (≥120°C)
rules.physicsNote: ATF oxidation doubles for every 10°C above 90°C. At 120°C, ATF life is reduced to ~50 hours. Clutch friction material degrades rapidly.

rules.name: Transmission Fluid High Temperature
rules.description: Transmission fluid in warning zone (100-120°C)
possibleCauses: Heavy towing or sustained gradient climb
rules.physicsNote: This range is recoverable but indicates cooling system marginal for current duty cycle. Long-term operation here degrades fluid faster than service intervals account for.

rules.name: Transmission Fluid Cold
rules.description: Transmission fluid below 10°C — cold shift quality affected, risk of varnish if operated hard
rules.physicsNote: ATF viscosity increases significantly below 10°C, causing engagement lag and pressure buildup issues. Designed for Kota Kinabalu highland use or air-conditioned parking structures.

rules.name: Limp Mode Active
rules.description: TCM has commanded limp home mode — severe internal fault detected
rules.triggerConditions: TCM limp mode DTC flag SET in TCM DTC data
rules.physicsNote: Limp mode restricts transmission to typically 2nd or 3rd gear at limited shift points. This is a protective strategy to allow vehicle movement while preventing further damage from uncontrolled shifting.

rules.name: High Torque Converter Slip
rules.description: Torque converter slip exceeding 15% at cruise — TC clutch failing to lock
rules.triggerConditions: TC Slip = (Input speed - Output speed) / Input speed > 15%
possibleCauses: Hydraulic pressure insufficient for TC lockup
rules.physicsNote: TC slip above 5% at highway cruise wastes significant fuel (TCC is designed to lock at cruise for ~3-5% fuel economy improvement). Above 15% generates substantial heat.

rules.name: Torque Converter Lockup Failure
rules.description: TC clutch unable to lock despite conditions exceeding lockup threshold (speed > 80 km/h, slip > 5%)
rules.triggerConditions: Speed > 80 km/h AND TC Slip > 5% sustained
possibleCauses: TC clutch plate worn beyond engagement
rules.physicsNote: At highway speed the torque converter should be fully locked (0% slip) for efficiency. Sustained slip at speed generates heat proportional to torque × slip speed — leading to fluid degradation.

rules.name: Gear Ratio Deviation
rules.description: Actual gear ratio deviating > 10% from nominal — clutch pack or band slipping
possibleCauses: Clutch pack wear in current gear
rules.physicsNote: Gear ratio is theoretically fixed by planetary gear tooth counts. Deviation indicates slippage in the clutch or band holding that gear — mechanical engagement quality issue.

rules.name: CVT Belt/Chain Slip
rules.description: CVT variator ratio fluctuating > 5% at steady state — belt/chain slipping on pulley
rules.triggerConditions: CVT ratio variance > 5% fluctuation at steady throttle/speed
possibleCauses: Clutch pack gripping the variator inconsistently
rules.physicsNote: CVT belts/chains transmit torque by clamping force on conical pulleys. Slip indicates insufficient clamping hydraulic pressure or belt wear. Progressive slip rapidly destroys belt and pulley running surfaces.
